summary: Toronto has adopted new rules to help tenants stay cool in rental buildings during hot weather. Starting June 1, 2026, apartment buildings with common areas must keep at least one shared space, like a lounge, no warmer than 26 degrees Celsius from June to September if individual units lack air conditioning. The city will also work on a future by-law to set a maximum indoor temperature for all rental units and continue its air conditioner assistance program for vulnerable residents.

This plan will make it easier for people to access financial help from the Hardship Fund, improve how cooling centres are communicated to those who need them most, and ensure these centres are more effectively activated during hot weather.

This decision reinforces the city's commitment to strongly fight any lawsuits that try to stop the development of much-needed housing and shelters across Toronto.

2023 Capital and Operating Budgets
summary: City Council has laid out its plans for the 2023 budget, proposing a 5.5 percent property tax increase and extending a special levy for city-building projects by 10 years. These funds will prioritize housing, community safety, and transit improvements, while the city also pushes provincial and federal governments for help with refugee and COVID-19 related costs.

title: Update on 2023 Toronto Public Health Operating Budget Submission and Service Levels
summary: Toronto's Board of Health decided to eventually release documents detailing Toronto Public Health's 2023 budget and proposed operational changes, but only after affected employees have been notified. Additionally, they asked for a review of current public health service levels, considering lessons learned from the COVID-19 pandemic. This ensures transparency on how public health funds are used and helps shape future health services for residents.

title: Election of Chair for 2023 - Board of Health
summary: Toronto's Board of Health has chosen Director Chris Moise to serve as its Chair for 2023. As Chair, he will lead the board in setting priorities and making key decisions on public health programs and services that affect all city residents.

title: 2023 Capital and Operating Budgets
2023 Capital and Operating Budgets
summary: The Budget Committee postponed a final decision on the city's 2023 annual budget. They moved the discussion to January 24, asking for more information on key areas such as 311 services, childcare, housing support, and the costs of city building maintenance and climate initiatives. This extra time allows for a deeper review of the budget, which funds all city services and determines property tax rates.

title: Implementing Bill 109, the More Homes for Everyone Act, 2022
summary: Responding to the Province's Bill 109, which aims to accelerate housing development, Toronto City Council approved measures to streamline its planning processes. This includes hiring 150 new staff to speed up development application reviews and shifting authority to city planners for certain approvals. Council also requested the Province delay a rule requiring the city to refund developer fees and to consult on future regulations impacting local development.

title: Operating Variance Report for the Nine Months Ended September 30, 2022
summary: City Council has officially asked the federal government for financial help to cover Toronto's 2022 budget deficit. Depending on whether this funding is provided, future property tax notices will inform residents about the federal government's contribution, or lack thereof, to the city's financial health.

summary: Addressing long-standing frustrations, Toronto City Council recently pressed the province and Metrolinx for accountability and financial relief over the Eglinton Crosstown LRT's significant construction delays. Council requested an independent review, detailed explanations for the issues, and compensation for residents and businesses, including potential property tax rebates, free bus fares along Eglinton, and funding for traffic wardens.

2023 Housing Action Plan
summary: Toronto City Council has approved a new Housing Action Plan to significantly increase home construction across the city, with a goal of building at least 285,000 new homes over the next decade. This plan involves reviewing zoning rules and other policies to make it easier to build all types of housing, including affordable options, and creating vibrant neighbourhoods where most daily needs are a short walk or bike ride away. The aim is to address Toronto's housing shortage and ensure a wider range of housing choices for residents.
